questions.question:
The overwhelming number of people infected with rabies in India has been flagged by the World Health Organization as a source of concern. It is estimated that inoculating 70% of pets and stray dogs against rabies can lead to a significant reduction in the number of people infected with rabies.

Which of the following can be most logically inferred from the above sentences?

A. The number of people in India infected with rabies is high.
B. The number of people in other parts of the world infected with rabies is low.
C. (i) and (ii) only.
D. (ii) and (iv) only.
questions.answer: A. The number of people in India infected with rabies is high.
questions.to_find: the most logical inference that can be drawn from the passage about rabies in India

understand.plain: The passage states that India has a very high number of rabies infections, enough to concern the WHO. It also mentions that vaccinating 70% of pets and stray dogs can significantly reduce human infections, implying dogs are the main transmission source. We need to identify what can be logically inferred — not assumed or extrapolated beyond what is stated.

keywords.explain: A conclusion drawn logically from given information, staying within what the text supports
keywords.example: If the WHO flags India's rabies numbers as concerning, we can infer those numbers are high

keywords.explain: Vaccinating animals to prevent disease transmission to humans
keywords.example: Inoculating 70% of stray dogs breaks the transmission chain to humans

keywords.explain: Extremely large in number, far exceeding what is typical or acceptable
keywords.example: The passage uses this word to describe rabies cases in India — directly supporting option A

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure reading comprehension question

given.aim: identify the statement most directly supported by the passage

terms.meaning: The World Health Organization has identified India's rabies infection numbers as a cause of concern
terms.example: WHO flags a situation only when it is significantly serious — implying numbers are high
terms.connects: directly supports option A about high infection numbers in India

terms.meaning: Vaccinating 70% of dogs (pets + strays) is estimated to significantly cut human rabies cases
terms.example: This establishes dogs as the primary vector for rabies transmission to humans
terms.connects: implies stray dogs are a main source, supporting inference about dog-related transmission

terms.meaning: An inference stays within what the text explicitly states; an assumption goes beyond it
terms.example: Saying 'numbers in other countries are low' is an assumption — the passage never mentions other countries
terms.connects: rules out option B which compares with other parts of the world not mentioned in the passage

given.plan: Check each option against the passage text. Option A is directly stated ('overwhelming number ... in India' + WHO concern). Option B extends to other countries not mentioned — it is an assumption, not an inference. Options C and D reference sub-statements (i) and (ii) which require evaluating specific numbered claims.

steps.title: Identify what the passage explicitly states
steps.apply: The passage explicitly says: (1) the number of rabies-infected people in India is 'overwhelming' and flagged by WHO, and (2) inoculating 70% of dogs can significantly reduce this. These two facts are stated directly.
steps.note: Stick strictly to what the passage says — do not add information from outside knowledge

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: Option A: 'The number of people in India infected with rabies is high' — directly supported by the word 'overwhelming' and WHO concern. This is the strongest inference. Option B: 'In other parts of the world the numbers are low' — the passage never mentions other countries, so this cannot be inferred. Options C/D reference numbered sub-statements that go beyond the two sentences provided.
steps.note: Logical inference must be grounded in passage text alone

steps.title: Select the correct answer
steps.apply: Option A is the only statement directly and logically supported by the passage without adding external information.

solution.result: A. The number of people in India infected with rabies is high.

questions.formula_note: Passage reading questions rely on logical inference skills, not mathematical formulas.

questions.question:
The old concert hall was demolished because of fears that the foundation would be affected by the construction of the new metro line in the area. Modern technology for underground metro construction tried to mitigate the impact of pressurized air pockets created by the excavation of large amounts of soil. But even with these safeguards, it was feared that the concert hall would not be stable.

From this, one can infer that:

A. the foundations of old buildings create pressurized air pockets underground, which are difficult to handle during metro construction.
B. metro construction has to be done carefully considering its impact on the foundations of existing buildings.
C. construction in an area from an impossible hurdle to metro construction in that area.
D. pressurized air can be used to excavate large amount of soil from underground areas.
questions.answer: B. metro construction has to be done carefully considering its impact on the foundations of existing buildings.
questions.to_find: the correct inference about metro construction and its impact on existing building foundations

understand.plain: The passage describes a real situation where an old concert hall was torn down because underground metro construction nearby threatened its foundation stability. Modern techniques were used to reduce the risk from pressurized air pockets caused by digging, but those safeguards were still deemed insufficient. The question asks for what this situation logically implies about metro construction in general.

keywords.explain: To reduce the severity or impact of something harmful
keywords.example: Engineers used modern technology to mitigate (lessen) the risk of pressurized air pockets damaging nearby foundations

keywords.explain: Pockets of trapped, compressed air that form when large amounts of soil are excavated underground
keywords.example: When a metro tunnel is bored, displaced soil creates pressure differences that can destabilize adjacent structures

keywords.explain: Protective measures taken to prevent damage or risk
keywords.example: Modern technology served as a safeguard but was still insufficient to guarantee the concert hall's safety

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; purely a reading comprehension passage

given.aim: identify what can be logically inferred about metro construction from the concert hall example

terms.term: concert hall demolished due to metro fears
terms.meaning: The building's foundation was at risk due to nearby underground construction, so it was preemptively torn down
terms.example: This shows that metro construction can directly threaten existing structures — even when safeguards exist
terms.connects: supports that metro construction needs care regarding nearby foundations (option B)

terms.meaning: Engineers applied the best available techniques to reduce the damage from soil excavation pressure
terms.example: Even best-practice technology was not enough — the risk remained significant
terms.connects: reinforces that care and caution are necessary, supporting option B

terms.term: not be stable even with safeguards
terms.meaning: The concert hall still faced structural instability despite all precautions taken
terms.example: This shows the severity of the problem and the need for careful planning in metro construction
terms.connects: eliminates option C (not an impossible hurdle — construction did proceed) and option D (pressurized air is a byproduct, not a tool)

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the passage. Option A reverses causality — it is the metro excavation that creates air pockets, not old building foundations. Option B is directly supported: the passage shows metro construction affects existing foundations and safeguards are needed. Option C overstates — buildings were demolished, metro construction was not called impossible. Option D misrepresents air pockets as a useful excavation tool rather than an unwanted side-effect.

steps.title: Identify the causal chain in the passage
steps.apply: Metro construction -> excavation of large soil -> pressurized air pockets -> risk to nearby foundations -> concert hall demolished. The direction of causality is clear: metro construction causes risk to foundations.
steps.note: Option A reverses this chain by saying old foundations create the air pockets — this is wrong

steps.title: Test option B against the passage
steps.apply: The passage says technology 'tried to mitigate' the impact and fears remained. This directly implies that metro construction must be done carefully (with safeguards) because it affects existing buildings. Option B captures this perfectly.
steps.note: This is a general inference drawn from a specific example — a valid logical step

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Option C: construction was not called 'impossible' — the hall was simply demolished as a precaution. Option D: pressurized air is a problem to manage, not a useful excavation method. Both options distort the passage meaning.

solution.result: B. metro construction has to be done carefully considering its impact on the foundations of existing buildings.

questions.formula_note: Reading comprehension questions require careful causal reasoning, not mathematical formulas.

questions.question:
Bhaichung was observing the pattern of people entering and leaving a car service centre. There was a single window where customers were being served. He saw that people inevitably came out of the centre in the order that they went in. However, the time they spent inside seemed to vary a lot: some people came out in a matter of minutes while others took a lot longer.

From this, what can one conclude?

A. The centre operates on a first-come-first-served basis, but with variable service times, depending on specific customers.
B. Customers were served in an arbitrary order, since they took varying amounts of time for service completion in the centre.
C. The time spent by a person inside the service centre was not ultimately associated with the actors, to get the perspective needed for imperial recording of matters.
D. Actors are typically associated with the imperial recording of matters.
questions.answer: A. The centre operates on a first-come-first-served basis, but with variable service times, depending on specific customers.
questions.to_find: the correct conclusion about the car service centre's operating pattern from the observations

understand.plain: Bhaichung notices two key facts: (1) customers always leave in exactly the same order they arrived — FIFO (first-in-first-out) discipline is strictly maintained, and (2) the time each customer spends inside is different — service time is variable. Together these two observations describe a classic FIFO queue with non-uniform service times. Options C and D are from a different passage and are irrelevant.

keywords.term: inevitably came out in the order they went in
keywords.explain: No matter what, customers exited in the same sequence as they entered — this is FIFO (First In, First Out) behaviour
keywords.example: If customers A, B, C entered in that order, they always left in the order A, B, C

keywords.explain: Different customers require different amounts of time to complete their service
keywords.example: A simple oil change takes 10 minutes; a full engine service takes 2 hours — both served in FIFO order

keywords.explain: A queue discipline where priority is given strictly to order of arrival
keywords.example: The observation that exit order always matches entry order confirms this queue discipline is in operation

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='10' width='510' height='140' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>ENTRY ORDER</text><rect x='20' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A</text><rect x='80' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='5' class='bg'/><text x='105' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>B</text><rect x='140' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='5' class='bg'/><text x='165' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>C</text><text x='210' y='80' class='big ink'>-></text><rect x='250' y='40' width='100' height='70' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SERVICE</text><text x='300' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>variable time</text><text x='370' y='80' class='big ink'>-></text><text x='400' y='40' class='lbl ink'>EXIT ORDER</text><rect x='400' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='425' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A</text><text x='460' y='55' width='50' height='40' class='sub mut'/><text x='20' y='130' class='sub mut'>Exit order always matches entry order (FIFO), but each person's service time varies.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing customers A, B, C entering in order, passing through a service window with variable time, and exiting in the same order A, B, C

given.aim: conclude the service discipline used at the car service centre based on Bhaichung's two observations

terms.meaning: Customers always leave in the exact same sequence they arrived, regardless of how long each takes
terms.example: Even if B takes 2 hours and A takes 10 minutes, B still exits after A because A arrived first
terms.connects: establishes that the queue is FIFO — first-come-first-served

terms.meaning: Different customers require different durations of service — no fixed service time
terms.example: Some customers finish in minutes, others take much longer
terms.connects: rules out the idea that the centre operates on a fixed schedule; service is customer-dependent

terms.meaning: Only one customer can be served at a time — pure sequential service
terms.example: No parallel processing — one window means strict queue ordering is observable and enforceable
terms.connects: explains why FIFO order is strictly maintained and observable

given.plan: Match the two observations (FIFO order + variable service time) to the options. Option A explicitly states both facts: first-come-first-served (FIFO) AND variable service times. Option B claims arbitrary order — contradicted directly by the observation. Options C and D appear to be from a different passage (about actors and imperial recording) and are clearly irrelevant to this passage.

steps.title: Extract the two key observations
steps.apply: Observation 1: People always come out in the same order they went in — this is FIFO (first-come-first-served). Observation 2: Time spent inside varies greatly — service time is variable per customer.
steps.note: Both observations must be captured in the correct answer

steps.title: Match observations to options
steps.apply: Option A states 'first-come-first-served basis' (matches observation 1) 'with variable service times' (matches observation 2) — perfect match. Option B claims 'arbitrary order' which directly contradicts observation 1. Options C and D discuss unrelated content about actors.
steps.note: Option A is the only choice that accurately accounts for both observations

steps.title: Confirm answer
steps.apply: The conclusion is that the centre uses FIFO service with variable per-customer service times. This matches Option A exactly.

solution.result: A. The centre operates on a first-come-first-served basis, but with variable service times, depending on specific customers.

questions.formula_note: Queue discipline (FIFO) is a conceptual observation — no mathematical formulas are needed for this inference question.

questions.question:
A map shows the elevations of Darjeeling, Gangtok, Kalimpong, Pelling, and Siliguri. Kalimpong is at a lower elevation than Gangtok. Pelling is at a higher elevation than Gangtok. Pelling is at a higher elevation than Kalimpong. Siliguri is at a lower elevation than Darjeeling. Darjeeling is at a lower elevation than Gangtok. Siliguri is at a lower elevation than Kalimpong.

Which of the following statements can be inferred from the paragraph above?

i. Pelling is at a higher elevation than Siliguri.
ii. Kalimpong is at a lower elevation than Darjeeling.
iii. Darjeeling is at a lower elevation than Kalimpong.
iv. Siliguri is at a lower elevation than Gangtok.

A. Only i.
B. Only ii and iii.
C. Only i and iv.
D. Only ii and iv.
questions.to_find: which statements about relative elevations of the five towns can be correctly inferred from the given relationships

understand.plain: This question gives a set of pairwise elevation comparisons between five towns and asks which derived statements logically follow. The key skill is chaining inequalities: if A > B and B > C, then A > C. We need to derive all possible orderings and check each statement carefully against the transitive chain.

keywords.explain: If A > B and B > C, we can infer A > C without being told directly
keywords.example: Pelling > Gangtok and Gangtok > Darjeeling implies Pelling > Darjeeling

keywords.explain: A numerical ranking of locations from lowest to highest altitude
keywords.example: Siliguri < Darjeeling < Gangtok < Pelling is a partial chain we can build from the clues

keywords.explain: When two quantities have no direct or transitive link in the given data, their comparison is unknown
keywords.example: If no chain connects Kalimpong to Darjeeling, we cannot infer which is higher

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='510' height='210' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Elevation (low to high) — known chain</text><rect x='30' y='155' width='80' height='35' rx='5' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='177' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Siliguri</text><rect x='140' y='120' width='80' height='35' rx='5' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Darjeeling</text><rect x='250' y='85' width='80' height='35' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='290' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Gangtok</text><rect x='250' y='145' width='80' height='35' rx='5' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='167' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Kalimpong</text><rect x='390' y='50' width='80' height='35' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='430' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Pelling</text><line x1='110' y1='172' x2='140' y2='155' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='137' x2='250' y2='102' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='330' y1='102' x2='390' y2='72' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='330' y1='162' x2='390' y2='72' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='205' class='sub mut'>Siliguri < Darjeeling < Gangtok < Pelling | Kalimpong < Gangtok < Pelling | Siliguri < Kalimpong</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Elevation diagram showing Siliguri at bottom, then Darjeeling, then Gangtok, with Kalimpong between Siliguri and Gangtok, and Pelling at top above both Gangtok and Kalimpong

given.aim: determine which derived elevation comparisons are logically valid given the stated pairwise relationships

terms.meaning: The six explicitly stated inequalities: Kalimpong < Gangtok; Pelling > Gangtok; Pelling > Kalimpong; Siliguri < Darjeeling; Darjeeling < Gangtok; Siliguri < Kalimpong
terms.example: These six facts are the only basis for inference — no external geography knowledge allowed
terms.connects: forms the complete set from which we derive the elevation ordering

terms.meaning: By chaining the inequalities transitively: Siliguri < Darjeeling < Gangtok < Pelling, and Siliguri < Kalimpong < Gangtok < Pelling
terms.example: Siliguri < Kalimpong (given), Kalimpong < Gangtok (given), Gangtok < Pelling (given) => Siliguri < Pelling (by transitivity)
terms.connects: enables checking all four statements in the question

terms.meaning: The text states Darjeeling < Gangtok and Kalimpong < Gangtok, and Siliguri < both, but never directly or transitively compares Darjeeling and Kalimpong
terms.example: Both Darjeeling and Kalimpong are below Gangtok, but their relative order is unspecified
terms.connects: makes statements ii (Kalimpong < Darjeeling) and iii (Darjeeling < Kalimpong) both unprovable from the given data

given.plan: Build the full transitive closure of all given inequalities. Chain: Siliguri < Darjeeling < Gangtok < Pelling, and Siliguri < Kalimpong < Gangtok < Pelling. Note Darjeeling vs Kalimpong is undetermined. Then check each of the four statements.

steps.title: List all given inequalities
steps.apply:
1. Kalimpong < Gangtok
2. Pelling > Gangtok (i.e., Gangtok < Pelling)
3. Pelling > Kalimpong (i.e., Kalimpong < Pelling)
4. Siliguri < Darjeeling
5. Darjeeling < Gangtok
6. Siliguri < Kalimpong
steps.note: Six direct relationships form our complete information set

steps.title: Build transitive chains
steps.apply:
Chain A: Siliguri < Darjeeling (4), Darjeeling < Gangtok (5), Gangtok < Pelling (2) => Siliguri < Darjeeling < Gangtok < Pelling
Chain B: Siliguri < Kalimpong (6), Kalimpong < Gangtok (1), Gangtok < Pelling (2) => Siliguri < Kalimpong < Gangtok < Pelling
Note: No chain connects Darjeeling and Kalimpong directly or transitively.
steps.note: Both Darjeeling and Kalimpong are below Gangtok but their mutual order is unknown

steps.title: Check each statement
steps.apply:
Statement i: Pelling > Siliguri — From Chain A or B: Siliguri < ... < Pelling. TRUE (can be inferred).
Statement ii: Kalimpong < Darjeeling — No chain establishes this. CANNOT be inferred.
Statement iii: Darjeeling < Kalimpong — No chain establishes this either. CANNOT be inferred.
Statement iv: Siliguri < Gangtok — From Chain A: Siliguri < Darjeeling < Gangtok. TRUE (can be inferred).
steps.note: Only statements i and iv are supported by the transitive closure of the given data

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Only i and iv are valid inferences. This corresponds to option C.

solution.result: C. Only i and iv. — Pelling is higher than Siliguri (i) and Siliguri is lower than Gangtok (iv) are both provable by transitive chaining of the given elevation relationships.

questions.formula_note: Elevation ordering uses transitive closure of inequalities — a logical reasoning technique, not a mathematical formula from the registry.
